params.text:
Dignity, privacy and respect

Dignity is at the heart of personal care. To act with dignity is to treat someone as a valued human being, whatever help they need. When you support a person to wash or dress, you are helping with something they would normally do alone and unseen, so small acts of thoughtfulness matter enormously.

  • Dignity: valuing the person and protecting their self-respect.
  • Privacy: keeping their body, their space and their information private.
  • Respect: treating their wishes, background and choices as important.

These values are not extra steps to fit in when there is time. They shape every part of good personal care, and they cost nothing but attention.

Protecting privacy in practice

Privacy is easy to overlook when you are busy, yet it is one of the things people say matters most to them. Simple actions protect it: closing doors and curtains, covering the parts of the body you are not washing, and knocking and waiting before you enter a room.

Privacy also means keeping what you learn about a person confidential. During personal care you may see or hear things they would not share widely — a scar, a worry, a moment of embarrassment. Treat all of it with discretion, sharing only what colleagues need to know to give safe care. Protecting a person's privacy tells them, without words, that you see them as a whole person and not just a task to complete.

params.question: A person is embarrassed about needing help to wash. Which approach best protects their dignity and privacy?

answers.text: Close the door and curtains, cover the parts of the body you are not washing, and explain each step as you go

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Privacy, keeping the person covered, and gentle explanation all protect their dignity.

answers.text: Work as quickly as possible and leave the door open so you can call for help

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Not quite. Leaving the door open exposes the person and does not respect their privacy.

answers.text: Chat to a colleague about the person's difficulties while you work

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Discussing the person's difficulties in front of them breaches confidentiality and dignity.

answers.text: Tell the person there is nothing to be embarrassed about and carry on without explaining

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Dismissing someone's feelings and not explaining what you are doing does not protect their dignity.
